finding: A framing manipulation (past-focused vs. future-focused) reduced disagreement with ideas, with effect mediated by nostalgia; the effect required peripheral processing and did not reverse for opposite groups.

Temporal perspective affected self-perception of rationality and emotionality asymmetrically: imagined future selves appeared more rational but less emotional than recalled past selves, with consequent differences in information-seeking behavior and task performance.
No finding of effect magnitude or replication: meta-analytic evidence indicates selection bias and reporting bias distorted a literature, reducing reliability of claimed effects.
Selective attention, typically beneficial for learning, can paradoxically create stable false beliefs when learners repeatedly encounter environments containing this attentional bias.
A bidirectional causal cycle emerged: experiencing disrespect heightened cynicism, and cynicism elicited disrespectful treatment from others, reinforcing the initial belief.
A dispositional attribute (political orientation) correlated with a neural marker of response conflict-sensitivity, with greater conflict activity in the more receptive group.
A visual cue (portion size visibility) increased intake volume, but did not affect perceived satiation or estimated intake; individuals relied on visual cues over internal signals.
A temporal attribute (age) produced divergent effects across social-cognitive and social-affective capacities: theory-of-mind declined but empathy remained stable or improved with aging.
An individual attribute (own age) subconsciously biased perception of an ambiguous figure toward age-congruent interpretations, despite participants' lack of awareness.
Visual eccentricity affected perception of emotional valence differently by emotion type: negative emotions showed greater degradation with distance from fixation than positive emotions.

An intervention (gameplay exposure to misinformation techniques) improved participants' ability to identify and resist misinformation, with effects independent of education, age, ideology, and cognitive style.
A syntactic constraint on language processing prevented detection of a simple perceptual pattern that non-syntactic alternatives revealed; syntax obligatorily dominated attention.
A framing manipulation (identity framing vs. behavior framing) of a survey item increased a downstream behavior (voting), with effects verified in official records.
An individual attribute (social class) predicted increased unethical behavior across multiple domains; attitudes toward greed partially mediated this effect.

Language comprehenders integrated semantic expectations and noisy sensory input according to Bayesian principles, with asymmetric treatment of insertion vs. deletion errors and sensitivity to noise rate.

Perceived intent motivated blame-related behavior even when controlling for blameworthiness judgments; motivation was evidenced by increased persistence and preference for blame over alternative tasks.
The possibility of third-party intervention in conflict increased cooperation regardless of third-party motivation; reducing third-party risk further increased intervention rates and cooperation.
A nonverbal display (postural expansiveness) increased attraction in zero-acquaintance contexts; the effect was mediated by perceptions of dominance.
An environmental attribute (economic inequality) moderated the relationship between income and generosity: higher-income individuals were less generous only under high inequality.

False beliefs about group-level biological differences predicted lower pain ratings for an outgroup target; these beliefs were partially endorsed by medical professionals and affected treatment recommendations.
